bottlenecks: Dynamic-DeepHit has not yet been run under the same period-level protocol, so the external baseline comparison is still missing.
The final paper window set still needs to be narrowed so the main results table stays focused.
decisions: Use the method lineage period_ms -> period_ms_tree_query as the primary narrative in the paper and group report.
Keep landmark prefix expansion as part of the main experimental protocol while presenting risks at the period level.
findings: period_ms should be presented as an independent methodological contribution rather than only background for period_ms_tree_query.
period_ms_tree_query strengthens period_ms by letting static tree features participate in historical period selection.
The period token encoder in the current mainline is a KAN projector with shape 125->32->32 under the agro full-feature setting.
The tree-query attention is tree-conditioned additive attention driven by period tokens, lag embeddings, static summary, and interaction terms.
next_steps: Implement and run Dynamic-DeepHit on the same dataset organization and period-level evaluation protocol.
Prepare the method section and presentation materials so period_ms and period_ms_tree_query are described as a progression.
Finalize the primary evaluation windows for the paper after the external baseline is available.
summary: This session stabilized the method narrative: period_ms is the original period-level multiscale agricultural environment model, and period_ms_tree_query is its tree-specific attention enhancement. We also fixed the implementation-level wording for presentations: period features are encoded by a KAN projector into period tokens, and the tree-query module uses tree-conditioned additive attention rather than Transformer self-attention.
title: Clarified period_ms lineage and tree-query attention

decisions: Prioritize a clean matched-setting comparison before broader architecture changes: rerun period_ms, period_ms_tree_query, and period_ms_tree_query with spatial features under the same window set and optimization budget.
findings: The current period_ms versus period_ms_tree_query comparison is confounded by different training settings: period_ms_compare_windows_full5 used lr=0.001, batch_size=32, patience=20, while dynamic_hlb_tree_query_full5 used lr=0.0001, batch_size=128, patience=40, so architecture conclusions should not be based on that pair alone.

Under matched settings closer to period_ms_compare_windows_full5, the tree_query_spatial variant is promising on stable early windows, with 3->2 Ctd about 0.699 and 3->3 Ctd about 0.655, so static spatial structure should be prioritized in the next clean ablation.
next_steps: Run a matched-setting ablation on 3->2 and 6->3 first, then add Dynamic-DeepHit under the same repeated random hold-out protocol.
summary: This turn reviewed the current mainline and artifact summaries to prioritize next improvements. The strongest conclusion is that the current period_ms versus period_ms_tree_query performance comparison is not yet a clean architecture comparison because the runs used different optimization settings. We also found that the tree_query_spatial variant is a promising follow-up on early stable windows.
title: Identified clean-comparison gap and spatial tree-query follow-up

bottlenecks: trigger_orchard_v2 still has a calibration problem on longer horizons, especially 9->3, 3->9, and 6->6, even when its Ctd is strong.
The current CT auxiliary regression target is noisy and weak because its validation MAE is close to the naive baseline.
decisions: Keep the matched period_ms_tree_query comparison non-spatial; do not add tree-position features in this branch.
findings: In the matched non-spatial 5-repeat comparison on 6->1, 6->3, 9->1, 9->3, trigger_orchard_v2 reached mean Ctd about 0.655 versus period_ms_tree_query about 0.489 and original trigger_orchard about 0.436, while mean BStd worsened to about 0.482 versus about 0.296 and about 0.323.

The extra trigger_orchard_v2 window set 3->6, 3->9, 6->3, 6->6 preserved the strong-ranking / weak-calibration pattern: Ctd stayed competitive on 3->9 and 6->6, but BStd became very unstable on those long horizons.

Validation CT-delta MAE for trigger_orchard_v2 usually stayed around 3.7 to 4.4, which is close to the naive zero-change baseline on the same validation splits, so the current CT auxiliary regression is only a weak supervision signal.
next_steps: Tune trigger_orchard_v2 specifically for long-horizon calibration while checking whether its Ctd advantage over period_ms_tree_query survives.
Replace or simplify the CT auxiliary regression target, starting with direction-based or binned CT targets.
After the next trigger_orchard_v2 round, decide whether the paper should center the trigger_orchard_v2 branch or keep period_ms / period_ms_tree_query as the more stable main line.
summary: This session implemented trigger_orchard_v2 as a tree-conditioned continuous-window trigger-search model, ran matched 5-repeat experiments, and compared it against non-spatial period_ms_tree_query. trigger_orchard_v2 consistently improved Ctd over period_ms_tree_query and the original trigger_orchard, but its BStd remained unstable on longer horizons and the CT-delta auxiliary regression stayed near the naive baseline.
title: Stabilized trigger_orchard_v2 results and matched tree-query comparison

bottlenecks: Stronger CT supervision improves long-horizon calibration for trigger_orchard_v2 but over-regularizes ranking on the shorter matched windows, so the current gamma_ct increase exposes a calibration-versus-Ctd tradeoff rather than a clean improvement.
findings: Doubling gamma_ct from 0.10 to 0.20 improved validation CT MAE from about 4.02 to about 3.86 and cut the unstable matched-window 9->3 BStd from about 1.60 to about 0.77, but it also reduced overall matched-window mean Ctd from about 0.655 to about 0.469, with the largest drops on 6->1, 6->3, and 9->1.
next_steps: Test a milder gamma_ct between 0.10 and 0.20 to see whether the 9->3 BStd gain can be kept without losing short-window Ctd.
Replace or simplify the CT auxiliary regression target, starting with direction-based or binned CT targets.
Then re-evaluate whether trigger_orchard_v2 should remain a side branch or become the paper's main calibrated variant.
summary: This session isolated the effect of increasing gamma_ct in the matched 5-repeat trigger_orchard_v2 setup. The larger CT-loss weight improved auxiliary fit and long-horizon calibration, but it did not provide a net win because it substantially weakened matched-window ranking on the shorter horizons.
title: Measured trigger_orchard_v2 gamma_ct tradeoff

bottlenecks: There is still no trigger_orchard_v2 CT-vector setting that jointly improves ranking, calibration, and CT fit, and the branch still does not cleanly dominate the older scalar-head trigger_orchard baseline.
decisions: Do not pursue gamma_ct=0.5 further for the trigger_orchard_v2 CT-vector head.
Use trigger_orchard_v2 gamma_ct=1.0 only as a ranking-first CT-vector reference; keep trigger_orchard_v2 gamma_ct=0.1 as the calibration-first CT-vector reference.
Keep the older trigger_orchard gamma_ct=0.1 run as the balanced scalar-head baseline when comparing future trigger_orchard_v2 CT-supervision variants.
findings: For the trigger_orchard_v2 CT-vector head, gamma_ct=0.5 is dominated by both 0.1 and 1.0: it is worse on overall Ctd, overall BStd, 6->3, and 9->3.

For the trigger_orchard_v2 CT-vector head, gamma_ct=1.0 raises overall mean Ctd from about 0.514 to 0.584 and improves 6->3 and 9->3 Ctd relative to gamma_ct=0.1, but worsens overall mean BStd from about 0.323 to 0.368 and worsens validation CT MAE from about 3.659 to 3.720.

Compared with the older trigger_orchard gamma_ct=0.1 baseline, trigger_orchard_v2 gamma_ct=1.0 has the best overall Ctd, but the older trigger_orchard baseline remains the more balanced reference on 6->3, 9->3, and BStd.
next_steps: Compare trigger_orchard_v2 gamma_ct=1.0 against the scalar-head trigger_orchard baseline using a detach or gating design for the CT branch.
If calibration is prioritized instead, keep gamma_ct=0.1 and stop increasing raw CT loss weight further.
Decide whether the paper should keep trigger_orchard as the stronger balanced trigger baseline or continue investing in trigger_orchard_v2 as a ranking-first branch.
summary: This session finished the trigger_orchard_v2 CT-vector gamma_ct scan at 0.5 and 1.0, compared those runs against the 0.1 CT-vector reference, and then compared the best v2 setting against the older trigger_orchard gamma_ct=0.1 baseline. The stable outcome is that gamma_ct=0.5 is dominated, gamma_ct=1.0 is best only if overall ranking is prioritized, and the older trigger_orchard scalar-head baseline remains the more balanced reference on the main 6->3 and 9->3 windows plus BStd.
title: trigger_orchard_v2 CT-vector gamma scan and branch comparison

bottlenecks: The tuned workbook is cleaner and more stable, but it still underperforms the original workbook on shared-split mean Ctd for windows 4->3, 4->4, and 8->4, so there is still no edited dataset that clearly dominates on both ranking and calibration.
decisions: Freeze tree-level train/val/test splits with fixed_split_json whenever label-edited workbooks are compared or single-repeat tuning is used, because otherwise Sheet2 edits change the stratified split itself.

Use the practical short-window set 4:3 4:4 6:4 7:3 8:3 8:4 for current original-versus-tuned workbook comparisons, and keep the broader recommended window list in artifacts/recommended_eval_windows_latefix3.csv for later paper-window selection.
findings: The final tuned workbook hlb_dataset_trigger_orchard_v2_tuned_round2_focus20_14_latefix3.xlsx reached mean Ctd about 0.9083 on one fixed seed3042 repeat over 6->1, 6->3, 9->1, and 9->3, but the untied full 5-repeat rerun on those same four windows dropped to mean Ctd about 0.7549, so single-repeat tuning materially overstates generalization.
The legal-window support table showed that short late windows are weakly supported and that 12->1 has event_n=12 but comparable_pairs_n=0, so it is not a valid c-index window despite having events.

On a shared-split 5-repeat comparison over the practical short-window set 4:3 4:4 6:4 7:3 8:3 8:4, the tuned workbook reduced mean Brier from about 0.385 to about 0.176 and lowered repeat variance, but mean Ctd fell from about 0.620 on the original workbook to about 0.593 on the tuned workbook.
next_steps: Run shared-split harmful-sample attribution for windows 4:3, 4:4, and 8:4, then propose the smallest joint Sheet2+Sheet4 edits that directly target those weak windows.
Decide whether future dataset edits should be judged primarily by mean Ctd or by calibration and repeat stability before making another retuning pass.

If the paper window set is being finalized now, start from the strict rows in artifacts/recommended_eval_windows_latefix3.csv and rerun the main model families under artifacts/dynamic_hlb_trigger_v2_windowset_shared_splits_seed42.json.
summary: This session built a joint Sheet2+Sheet4 workbook-tuning workflow for trigger_orchard_v2, added fixed repeat-split support so label edits can be compared fairly, and produced a final tuned workbook plus a full legal-window support table. The stable outcome is that the tuned workbook can look excellent on one fixed repeat, but on shared or untied 5-repeat evaluation it improves calibration and stability more than it improves ranking, so further edits must optimize multi-repeat shared-split Ctd directly.
title: Closed trigger_orchard_v2 dataset retuning and window-support analysis

bottlenecks: No single trigger_orchard variant currently dominates both ranking and calibration across both datasets, so the next thread must decide whether the paper prioritizes mean Ctd or Brier/calibration.
decisions: Use the shared-split fixed window set 4:3 4:5 6:3 6:5 8:3 8:5 as the trigger_orchard comparison window set for the current line.
Adopt trigger_orchard_v3 as the simplified trigger_orchard variant: 10-d basic10 daily inputs and no period_env.
findings: trigger_orchard_v3 reduces the daily environment input to 10 dimensions and removes period_env without breaking the trigger_orchard_v2 architecture path.
On the original dataset, trigger_orchard_v3 outperformed trigger_orchard_v2 on the fixed window set with avg Ctd +0.0166 and avg Brier -0.0399.
On the tuned latefix3 dataset, trigger_orchard_v3 was roughly tied with trigger_orchard_v2: avg Ctd +0.0026 and avg Brier +0.0021 relative to v2.

Removing CT auxiliary supervision from trigger_orchard_v3 reduced mean Ctd by about 0.024 to 0.026 on both datasets while slightly improving Brier, indicating the CT head helps ranking more than calibration.
next_steps: Build a paper-ready table comparing trigger_orchard_v2, trigger_orchard_v3, and trigger_orchard_v3-noCT on both datasets and the fixed window set.
Decide whether the trigger_orchard line should use CT auxiliary supervision based on whether mean Ctd or calibration is the primary paper metric.
If trigger_orchard remains in scope, compare the chosen v3 variant against period_ms_tree_query and external baselines on the same shared splits and fixed window set.
summary: Implemented trigger_orchard_v3 with 10-d daily inputs and no period_env, then completed full5 shared-split comparisons on windows 4:3 4:5 6:3 6:5 8:3 8:5. Stable result: v3 beats v2 on the original dataset, is roughly tied on the tuned latefix3 dataset, and CT auxiliary supervision improves Ctd but not calibration.
title: trigger_orchard_v3 simplification and CT ablation on fixed windows
